mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned GLK petrol engine (M274.920) typically costs £1,800–£4,000 supply only depending on power output (GLK 200 or GLK 250). Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. A new OEM engine replacement can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274 is a refined, efficient engine with a proven track record of reliability across the Mercedes range. However, it has known weaknesses: thermostat housing leaks (the most common issue), camshaft actuator wear on pre‑2014 engines, timing chain stretch on neglected oil intervals, and occasional HPFP/turbocharger issues at high mileage. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are thermostat housing leaks (coolant loss, overheating risk), camshaft actuator wear (rattling on cold start, pre‑2014 engines), timing chain stretch (neglected oil intervals), and occasional high‑pressure pump/turbocharger issues at high mileage. Thermostat failure is the most common issue on the M274.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a GLK petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £5,000–£12,000 GLK, a £1,800–£4,000 recon is 15‑50% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the timing chain has snapped or the engine has suffered catastrophic failure, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my GLK's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The GLK 200 and GLK 250 petrol models both use the M274.920 engine code – a 2.0‑litre turbocharged inline‑four. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The GLK 200 produces 184 PS; the GLK 250 produces 211 PS.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a GLK petrol engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – thermostat housing leaks, camshaft actuator wear, timing chain stretch (if caught early), HPFP/turbocharger issues – are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped or the engine has suffered major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the GLK 200/250 petrol pricing. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. A new OEM engine replacement can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. Labour costs for engine replacement typically range from £800 to £2,500 depending on vehicle complexity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLK petrol engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The GLK's longitudinal engine layout and 4MATIC all‑wheel‑drive system (where fitted) add some complexity.

problems.title: M274 Thermostat Housing Leaks & Thermostat Failure
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, engine warning light, sweet smell of antifreeze, poor heater performance, temperature gauge fluctuations. Thermostat failure is the most common issue on the M274 engine.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 [PLACEHOLDER] (thermostat housing replacement — coolant refill and bleeding required).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can cause head gasket failure).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss or overheating occurs.

problems.recommendation: The M274's thermostat housing is a known weak point. The housing can develop cracks or seal failures, causing coolant leaks. Thermostat failure is considered the most common issue on the M274. If you notice coolant loss, a sweet smell, or temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system inspected immediately. Regular coolant changes (every two years) are essential to prevent corrosion.

problems.title: Camshaft Actuator Wear (Pre‑2014 Engines)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or ticking noise from the engine on cold start, rough idle, engine warning light, loss of power. Camshaft actuators (solenoids) can wear, typically after 60‑70k miles.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored wear can cause timing issues).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear unusual noises on cold start.

problems.recommendation: Pre‑2014 M274 engines can suffer from camshaft actuator wear. Symptoms include rattling or ticking noises on cold start. If you hear unusual noises, have the camshaft actuators inspected. Replacement is relatively straightforward and cheaper than letting the issue escalate. Regular oil changes (using the correct MB‑spec oil) are essential to prevent premature wear.

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (Neglected Oil Intervals)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, loss of power. Timing chain stretch can occur on neglected oil intervals.
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£4,000 [PLACEHOLDER] (if chain snaps or jumps and damages the engine).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The M274's timing chain can stretch if oil changes are neglected. Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) using the correct MB‑spec oil are essential to prevent chain wear. If you hear rattling on cold start, have the timing system inspected immediately. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

problems.title: High‑Pressure Fuel Pump & Turbocharger Issues (High Mileage)
problems.symptoms: Hard starting, loss of power, engine warning light, rough idle, misfires, reduced fuel economy.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hard starting or loss of power occurs.

problems.recommendation: At higher mileages, the M274 can occasionally exhibit problems with the high‑pressure pump and turbocharger. Regular use of high‑quality fuel and timely fuel filter changes can help prevent HPFP issues. If you experience hard starting or loss of power, have the fuel system and turbocharger inspected.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Catch it early — cheaper than engine replacement
Full engine replacement — valve damage is likely
Worth fixing — common on high‑mileage engines
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* No signs of coolant loss — thermostat housing in good condition
* Timing chain quiet — no rattling on cold start
* A well‑maintained example — the M274 is a proven, reliable engine
avoidIf: * Any sign of coolant loss or overheating — thermostat housing warning
* Any sign of rattling on cold start — timing chain or camshaft actuator warning
* No evidence of regular oil changes — the M274 is intolerant of neglect
* Poor service history — these engines demand strict maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes GLK 200 and 250 petrol models are powered by the M274 2.0‑litre turbocharged inline‑four — a refined, efficient engine with a proven track record across the Mercedes range. The GLK 200 offers 184 PS and excellent fuel economy; the GLK 250 offers 211 PS and 350 Nm for a more spirited drive. Both engines are fundamentally reliable, but have known weaknesses: thermostat housing leaks (the most common issue), camshaft actuator wear on pre‑2014 engines, timing chain stretch on neglected oil intervals, and occasional HPFP/turbocharger issues at high mileage. Buy on service history above all else — a well‑maintained GLK with the M274 is a reliable and capable SUV; a neglected example can be expensive to repair.

cta.label: → Compare GLK Petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes GLK 200 / 250 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Coolant Level — Any sign of coolant loss? (thermostat housing warning)
2. Cold Start — Any rattling or ticking? (camshaft actuator or timing chain warning)
3. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
4. Engine Serial Number — Check if pre‑2014 (camshaft actuator issue more common)
5. Temperature Gauge — Does it stay stable? (thermostat warning)
6. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any thermostat, timing, or fuel system codes?
7. Running Quality — Any rough idle, hesitation, or hard starting? (HPFP warning)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes

If the timing chain has snapped or the engine has suffered catastrophic failure, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing.
items.value: 5. How do I find my GLK's engine code?

items.label: The GLK 200 and GLK 250 petrol models both use the M274.920 engine code — a 2.0‑litre turbocharged inline‑four. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The GLK 200 produces 184 PS; the GLK 250 produces 211 PS.
items.value: 6. Can I repair a GLK petrol engine issue, or does it need replacement?

items.label: Most issues — thermostat housing leaks, camshaft actuator wear, timing chain stretch (if caught early), HPFP/turbocharger issues — are repairable. However, if the timing chain has snapped or the engine has suffered major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

items.label: schema, tags, and structure.
items.value: Alright, for Part 2 of this GLK page, I need to follow the established template from the companion files. That means generating Section 9 (Trust + Final CTA), meta titles/descriptions, Open Graph tags, Twitter Card tags, and most importantly, the complete JSON-LD schema stack.

items.label: The user provided the Part 1 output, so I have all the data I need: brand (Mercedes-Benz), domain (mercedesengine.uk), specialist count (25+), scrap threshold (70%), CTA mode (Consultative from Part 1's Step 0), the Era Map rows (GLK 200 and GLK 250 both with M274.920), and the engine code.
items.value: I need to make sure the schema matches the Part 1 Era Map exactly. The Service schema's price range should span the full history from the lowest used (£1,200) to the highest rebuilt (£4,000) as listed in the Part 1 summary. I should also include the standard blocks: BreadcrumbList, two ItemLists (for Era Map and Engine Codes), FAQPage with the six questions from Part 1, Organization, Service, Dataset, and WebPage. AggregateRating should be omitted as usual.

items.label: The meta title and description need to fit the character limits and include the key details from the page. The trust points should be tailored to the specific engine and its known issues (thermostat housing, camshaft actuator, timing chain).
items.value: The Production Note is crucial—it's a quality assurance step. I need to confirm consistency between the content and schema, state that AggregateRating is omitted, justify the price range, and confirm the Authority Model logic (that the rating was newly assigned based on live sources). I should list some of those sources to show the work was done.

items.label: Finally, I'll wrap up with a clear "VARIANT PAGE COMPLETE" message, summarizing what was delivered.
items.value: The user's deep need is for a reliable, automated system that produces high-quality, consistent, and accurate content at scale. My response needs to demonstrate that I understand and can execute this complex workflow flawlessly every time.
